ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Dum Aloo exists; mapped to the broader 'potato dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Dum Aloo; A0CKP (cooked potato dish) used as the closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific Indian potato curry preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant or home serving of Dum Aloo is approximately 300 g (one bowl), containing roughly 4–5 baby potatoes in a thick spiced gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN) / ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Dum Aloo is not listed as a single entry in NIN tables. Values are adapted from NIN data for cooked potato preparations, yogurt-based gravies, and standard Indian spice blends. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for fried potato and dairy components. Micronutrient values (vitamins, minerals) are consistent with NIN composite data for similar potato-based curry dishes.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — potato-based dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dum Aloo as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled/fried white potatoes (GI ~70–85), moderated by the presence of yogurt and fat which slow gastric emptying. A conservative mid-range value of 72 is applied. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 40.0) / 100 = 28.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (283 mg per 100 g; ~850 mg per 300 g serving) from added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Dum Aloo to occasional consumption, request reduced-salt preparation, and pair with potassium-rich low-sodium sides to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) and significant starch content from deep-fried baby potatoes leading to rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to no more than 150 g, pair with high-fibre vegetables or legumes to blunt the glycemic response, and monitor blood glucose after consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.67 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium from deep-frying and yogurt-based gravy preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Dum Aloo to small portions, prefer air-fried or baked potato variants, and choose low-fat yogurt in preparation to reduce saturated fat and overall caloric density.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potatoes belong to the nightshade family and may trigger inflammatory sensitivity in some RA patients, though fennel and ginger have anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor personal tolerance to nightshade vegetables and consider reducing frequency if joint symptoms worsen after consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dum Aloo is a celebrated North Indian and Kashmiri potato curry in which small baby potatoes are first pricked with a fork, deep-fried until golden and slightly crisp, and then slow-cooked ('dum') in a sealed vessel using steam and pressure to allow the potatoes to absorb a deeply spiced gravy. The Kashmiri version is distinguished by a yogurt-and-fennel-seed base with Kashmiri red chilli, producing a vibrant brick-red sauce with a warming, mildly tangy flavour. The Punjabi version uses a richer onion-tomato masala often finished with cream. The texture of the finished dish is soft and yielding inside with a slightly firm skin, bathed in a thick, aromatic, oil-enriched gravy. Nutritionally, Dum Aloo provides approximately 150 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (3.5 g), significant fat from frying oil and dairy (9.3 g), and starchy carbohydrates (13.3 g). Sodium is notably high at 283 mg per 100 g, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index is high (~72), driven by the potato base, making portion control essential for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-suitable. It is commonly served as a main course at lunch or dinner, often alongside roti, naan, or steamed rice, and is a popular choice at celebratory meals and restaurant dining across Northern India and the Indian diaspora.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Dum Aloo?
Is Dum Aloo good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Kashmiri and Punjabi Dum Aloo?
Is Dum Aloo high in sodium?
Can I eat Dum Aloo if I have high blood pressure?
What spices are in Dum Aloo?

confidence_reason: Dum Aloo is not listed as a single composite entry in NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from NIN data for cooked potato preparations and yogurt-based gravies, with cross-referencing to USDA SR Legacy for fat and cholesterol components. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney potato data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Dum Aloo preparation is not available in NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in available approved sources. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per NIN data for this plant-based preparation with minimal dairy; potatoes and spices do not contribute meaningful vitamin D. trans_fat_g of 0.07 g per 100 g reflects trace levels from deep-frying oil; this is a plausible trace value consistent with USDA data for fried potato products. Portions deduplicated from input: two distinct portion sizes retained — 1 bowl (300 g) and 1 serving (250 g); duplicate entries removed. GI source is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Dum Aloo as a composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 300 g: GL = (72 × 13.33 × 3) / 100 = 28.8.
